generated_comment: For us, it's really about what's practical. My husband handles all the investments and external financial matters – he's brilliant with that. And I'm just naturally better at organizing the house, the kids' schedules, making sure everything runs smoothly on the domestic front. It just made sense to divide it this way, jo jahan better hai, you know? He's incredibly supportive, of course; he always takes the kids for their weekend sports and helps out wherever he can.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, from a highly successful professional woman, rationalizes a traditional gendered division of labor as 'practical' and 'natural' ('jo jahan better hai'). It subtly frames domestic management as her innate responsibility while portraying her husband's limited involvement as 'support' and 'helping out', which are typical surface manifestations of the 'he helps her' framing and celebration of minimal male contribution. It reinforces the core belief that internal household management is a woman's domain.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 44. A woman who is the VP of operations at a large company in Mumbai. You have been in the workforce for 20 years. You have two children. You are speaking on a panel at a corporate leadership event. The moderator asks you how you have managed career and family. Your answer is polished and practised. But underneath the polish is a set of assumptions about what women owe their families that you have never examined because examining them would require you to look at what the question itself is doing Ñ why it is only ever asked of women on panels, why it is never followed by the equivalent question for the male panellists sitting next to you, and what it means that your most asked question is not about your work but about how you have arranged your domestic life around your work.
